title: NIS2 Art.23(4)(a) ŌĆö 24-Hour Early-Warning Notification Readiness
industry: All sectors in NIS2 Annex I and II
exclusions: Does not classify whether the incident actually meets the Art.23(1) significance threshold (the lookup encodes the operator's pre-classified mapping; classification is a legal determination by the IR lead). Does not draft the early-warning notice text, does not submit the notice to the competent authority or CSIRT, does not establish the operator's NIS2 entity scope, and does not replace counsel review of cross-border, sectoral, or special-status notification channels.
evidence: Saved search `UC-22.2.1` row-per-finding output, `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:saved_search` archive (7-year retention, RFC 3161 TSA-signed via `evidenceSigning`), and the `NIS2 Art.23 ŌĆö 24h clock` panel on dashboard `compliance/nis2-art23-posture.xml`. Daily PDF digest delivered to `head-of-ir@<entity>` and `csirt-liaison@<entity>` archived to the same evidence index.

compliance.assurance_rationale: This UC addresses Art.23(4)(a) specifically ŌĆö the 24-hour early-warning obligation ŌĆö and thereby contributes to the overall Art.23 incident-notification framework.
compliance.controlObjective: Contributes evidence toward the Art.23 incident-reporting lifecycle by monitoring the 24-hour early-warning clock for significant incidents.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search UC-22.2.1 row-per-finding output archived to index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:saved_search.

compliance.assurance_rationale: The UC produces auditor-ready Splunk evidence that the SOC has visibility on the 24-hour early-warning clock for every significant incident. Legal classification and the actual filing to the CSIRT/competent authority remain outside Splunk and require human approval, hence partial.
compliance.controlObjective: Proves that for every significant incident raised in ES, the 24-hour early-warning clock is monitored, an owner is assigned, and the early-warning package is drafted within deadline.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search `UC-22.2.1` writing one row per high/critical notable approaching or breaching the 24-hour clock to `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:saved_search`, signed via RFC 3161 TSA. Daily digest archived to `index=audit_evidence` with 7-year retention. Dashboard panel `NIS2 Art.23 ŌĆö 24h clock` on the NIS2 posture dashboard.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Fixture `sample-data/uc-22.2.1-fixture.json` injects a notable with `rule_name="NIS2-Significant-Ransomware"`, `urgency="critical"`, `status="In Progress"`, `_time=now()-21h`, lookup match `nis2_significant="yes"`, no exception. The search returns one row with `clock_state="WARN_T_MINUS_4H"`, `hours_since_detection`Ōēł21, and a populated `evidence_id`.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Same fixture but with `status="Closed"` and a closure timestamp at `now()-19h`, OR with a non-expired entry in `nis2_exception_register.csv` for the rule_name. The search returns 0 rows. Verifies that closed notables and time-bound approved exceptions correctly suppress.

dataSources: ES `notable` macro (resolves to `index=notable eventtype=notable` in ES <7.0 or the KV-store-backed `incident_review` collection in ES 7.0+). Auto-extracted fields: `rule_name`, `urgency` (informational/low/medium/high/critical), `status` (Unassigned/New/In Progress/Pending/Resolved/Closed), `owner`, `status_description`, `comment`, `risk_object`, `risk_object_type`, `src`, `dest`, `category`, `severity`, `risk_score`, `_time` (notable creation time). Optional join: `index=phantom sourcetype=phantom:container container_id=*` for SOAR cases linked via `nis2_incident_link.csv`. Reference lookup: `nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv` (clause-tier threshold mapping per the operator's NIS2 self-classification).
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263) Ōēź7.0; Splunk Add-on for Phantom (Splunkbase 3411) Ōēź3.0 for SOAR enrichment; Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621) Ōēź5.3 for the Alerts data model.
spl:
`notable` urgency IN ("high","critical") earliest=-3d
| eval hours_since_detection = round((now() - _time) / 3600, 2)
| lookup nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv rule_name OUTPUT nis2_tier nis2_significant
| eval nis2_significant = coalesce(nis2_significant, "unclassified")
| where nis2_significant != "no" AND status != "Closed"
| eval clock_state = case(
    hours_since_detection >= 24, "BREACH",
    hours_since_detection >= 20, "WARN_T_MINUS_4H",
    hours_since_detection >= 12, "NOTICE_T_MINUS_12H",
    1==1, "OK")
| where clock_state != "OK"
| lookup nis2_exception_register.csv rule_name OUTPUT exception_valid_until
| where isnull(exception_valid_until) OR strptime(exception_valid_until, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S") < now()
| eval evidence_id = sha256(rule_name . event_id . _time)
| eval owner = coalesce(owner, "UNASSIGNED")
| table _time, rule_name, urgency, nis2_tier, status, owner, hours_since_detection, clock_state, src, dest, risk_object, evidence_id
| sort - hours_since_detection
description: Detects ES notables raised at high or critical urgency that map to a NIS2-significant incident threshold and are approaching or have breached the Art.23(4)(a) 24-hour early-warning clock measured from notable creation, after excluding any time-bounded approved exceptions.
value: Missing the 24-hour early-warning window forfeits the supervisory authority's good-faith presumption under Art.32(4)(a) and triggers the Annex II inquiry obligations. This UC gives the Head of IR an auditable clock per significant incident so the early-warning is drafted before the deadline, not reconstructed afterwards.
implementation: (1) Onboard the `nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv` lookup mapping each correlation `rule_name` to its NIS2 tier (Annex I/II/Implementing Reg 2024/2690 thresholds). (2) Schedule the SPL every 15 minutes over the last 3 days. (3) Configure `evidence_id` summary indexing to `audit_evidence`. (4) Send PagerDuty page on `clock_state=WARN_T_MINUS_4H`, escalate on `BREACH`. (5) Throttle by `rule_name` for 4 hours so a single noisy rule does not re-page during one campaign.
visualization: Row 1: Single-value tile `Significant incidents in clock window` (red Ōēź1). Row 2: Timeline panel showing notable aging with three threshold bands (12h, 20h, 24h). Row 3: Sortable table (rule_name, urgency, owner, hours_since_detection, status, drilldown to ES Incident Review for the notable). Row 4: 14-day trellis of `clock_state` counts per `nis2_tier` for capacity-review meetings.
cimSpl:
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Alerts.Alerts where Alerts.severity IN ("high","critical") AND Alerts.status!="closed" AND _time >= relative_time(now(), "-3d") by Alerts.severity, Alerts.signature, Alerts.owner, Alerts._time
| rename Alerts.* as *
| eval hours_since_detection = round((now() - _time) / 3600, 2)
| where hours_since_detection >= 12
| sort - hours_since_detection
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration on the `Alerts` data model with a 7-day retention. The cimSpl variant is 5ŌĆō20├Ś faster than the raw macro at fleet steady state because the `notable` macro joins against the `incident_review` KV collection per row; the DMA pre-aggregates this. Use the raw macro variant only when investigating an outlier and you need the un-aggregated `comment` / `status_description` text.

references.title: Splunk ES Notable Event Framework documentation
Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2024/2690 ŌĆö significant incident thresholds
ENISA NIS2 Technical Implementation Guidance ŌĆö incident notification

knownFalsePositives: 1) **Active major incident with declared crisis state** ŌĆö analysts deliberately leave many notables open while triaging the underlying campaign. Distinguish: an open ServiceNow MAJ ticket and `risk_score > 250` on the parent risk object in `index=risk` for the same time window. Suppress: open a single time-bound exception in `nis2_exception_register.csv` keyed on the parent campaign rule_name for the duration of the declared crisis; do not suppress the rule globally. 2) **ES Risk-Based Alerting consolidating individual rule fires into an aggregate notable** ŌĆö per-rule notables disappear and only the aggregate `Risk Analysis` notable fires. Distinguish: `urgency=informational` on the per-rule notable but `index=risk` shows recent contributions for that rule_name. Suppress: pivot the SPL to count risk-aggregated notables instead via the `Risk` data model. 3) **Bulk re-tuning after correlation-search release** ŌĆö analysts re-triage while the rule's `lastModified` is recent. Distinguish: `| rest /servicesNS/-/SA-ThreatIntelligence/saved/searches | search title=<rule_name> | table updated`. Suppress: 7-day time-bound exception in `nis2_exception_register.csv`. 4) **Holiday weekend / unfilled on-call shift** ŌĆö no analyst has acknowledged. Distinguish: cross-check `index=pagerduty sourcetype=pagerduty:incident` for an unacknowledged page tied to the notable. Do NOT suppress ŌĆö the aged notable is a legitimate IR-drill finding and should escalate the on-call schedule. 5) **Notable raised via the `Risk Analysis` correlation search at urgency=informational** ŌĆö collected for context, not paged. Distinguish: `urgency="informational"`. Suppress: filter `urgency IN ("high","critical")` in the SPL (already enforced). 6) **Forwarder clock skew on the indexer that received the notable** ŌĆö `_time` is hours behind real time. Distinguish: `| eval lag=_indextime-_time | where lag > 1800` returns rows. Investigate via `chronyc sources` on the source. Suppress: use `_indextime` instead of `_time` for the clock calculation only when forwarder skew is documented in `nis2_known_skew_hosts.csv`.
equipmentModels: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928, incident and change request data)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263, notable events and incident response workflow)
Splunk SOAR 6.x (Splunkbase 3411, automated playbook for notification workflow)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

ŌĆó **Splunk**: Enterprise Ōēź9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
ŌĆó **Lookups**: `nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv`, `nis2_exception_register.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv','nis2_exception_register.csv')`.
ŌĆó **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.23`. The control objective is: "Contributes evidence toward the Art.23 incident-reporting lifecycle by monitoring the 24-hour early-warning clock for significant incidents." ŌĆö every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 ŌĆö Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC ŌĆö no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into the relevant indexes under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


ŌĆó **Hint 1:** Onboard the `nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv` lookup mapping each correlation `rule_name` to its NIS2 tier (Annex I/II/Implementing Reg 2024/2690 thresholds). This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability ŌĆö document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

ŌĆó **Hint 2:** Schedule the SPL every 15 minutes over the last 3 days. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

ŌĆó **Hint 3:** Configure `evidence_id` summary indexing to `audit_evidence`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability ŌĆö document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

ŌĆó **Hint 4:** Send PagerDuty page on `clock_state=WARN_T_MINUS_4H`, escalate on `BREACH`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability ŌĆö document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

ŌĆó **Hint 5:** Throttle by `rule_name` for 4 hours so a single noisy rule does not re-page during one campaign. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability ŌĆö document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 ŌĆö Implement the search

For "NIS2 Art.23(4)(a) ŌĆö 24-Hour Early-Warning Notification Readiness," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the NIS2 control ŌĆö each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This detection correlates event arrival time with a business timestamp from `exception_valid_until` (format: `%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S`). The `strptime()` conversion to epoch is the linchpin ŌĆö the subsequent `where` clause compares Splunk's `_time` against the parsed epoch to detect policy violations (activity after a termination, events past a retention boundary, operations outside a maintenance window, etc).

Silent failure mode: if `exception_valid_until` arrives in a format the `strptime` call doesn't match, the conversion returns null, propagates through the `where` comparison as 'unknown,' and the row is dropped from results. This produces under-reporting, not over-reporting ŌĆö which is the dangerous direction for a compliance detection. Validate by running:

```spl
<base search>
| eval exception_valid_until_epoch = strptime(exception_valid_until, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S")
| where isnull(exception_valid_until_epoch)
| stats count by exception_valid_until
```

If this returns rows, the upstream system has multiple timestamp formats (common with SaaS connectors that change during version upgrades) and the `strptime` call must be hardened with `coalesce(strptime(exception_valid_until, "fmt1"), strptime(exception_valid_until, "fmt2"))` to handle them all.

Time-zone alignment: if the source system emits `exception_valid_until` in a different zone from the Splunk search head, the comparison will be offset. Look for `%z` or `%Z` in the format string ŌĆö if absent, the conversion uses `TZ = TZ of Splunk host`, which is correct only when source and target agree. Search covers the last 3 days.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Alerts` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Alerts.Alerts where Alerts.severity IN ("high","critical") AND Alerts.status!="closed" AND _time >= relative_time(now(), "-3d") by Alerts.severity, Alerts.signature, Alerts.owner, Alerts._time
| rename Alerts.* as *
| eval hours_since_detection = round((now() - _time) / 3600, 2)
| where hours_since_detection >= 12
| sort - hours_since_detection
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Alerts` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC ŌĆö the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.2.1: NIS2 Art.23(4)(a) ŌĆö 24-Hour Early-Warning Notification Readi]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -3d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.2.1,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 ŌĆö Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data ŌĆö it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `_time`, `rule_name`, `urgency`, `nis2_tier`, `status`, `owner`, `hours_since_detection`, `clock_state`, `src`, `dest`. Run:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table _time, rule_name, urgency, nis2_tier, status, owner, hours_since_detection, clock_state
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `hours_since_detection`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(hours_since_detection) as p50 perc90(hours_since_detection) as p90 perc99(hours_since_detection) as p99 max(hours_since_detection) as hi
```

The current threshold `24` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 ŌĆö Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.2.1: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 ŌĆö Row 1: Single-Value Tile `Significant Incidents In Clock Window` (Red Ōēź1). Row 2: Timeline Panel Showing Notable Aging With Three Threshold Bands (12H, 20H, 24H). Row 3: Sortable Table (Rule Name, Urgency, Owner, Hours Since Detection, Status, Drilldown To Es Incident Review For The Notable). Row 4: 14-Day Trellis Of `Clock State` Counts Per `Nis2 Tier` For Capacity-Review Meetings (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y ŌĆö the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 ŌĆö Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Silent under-reporting:** The `strptime` conversion is likely failing on a subset of events ŌĆö see Step 3 for the hardening procedure. Check for format drift after upstream system upgrades.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

ŌĆó 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours ŌĆö the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

ŌĆó Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

ŌĆó Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

ŌĆó Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) ŌĆö these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.
grandmaExplanation: We watch every serious cyber alert and start a 24-hour clock the moment it is raised, so the team can warn the regulator before the clock runs out ŌĆö not after, when it is too late.
